behaviors.description: Files placed in subdirectories of the watched root are processed, not only root-level files
behaviors.justification: The failure is in watcher configuration: chokidar/fs.watch not set to recurse into subdirectories. A Unit test with mocked filesystem would pass even if the watcher configuration is wrong. Real filesystem with real watcher exposes the misconfiguration.
behaviors.test_description: Create daemon watching /tmp/test, write file to /tmp/test/subdir/file.txt, verify daemon processes it and writes to output. Repeating with different subdirectory depths would catch glob pattern errors.

behaviors.description: Deduplication correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window
behaviors.justification: Real failure: same file path generates two filesystem events within the dedup window (e.g., 100ms). Unit test of dedup logic alone wouldn't catch if event handling bypasses dedup. Integration test with real filesystem events and timing exposes the full event→dedup→process pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Watch /tmp/test with dedup window of 50ms. Write a file, trigger rapid rescan/event (e.g., chmod or metadata refresh). Verify file is processed only once. Could use inotify/fs.watch instrumentation to observe raw events.

behaviors.description: When OS emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once
behaviors.justification: The failure is OS-specific: Windows, macOS, Linux generate different numbers of events (1–3+) for a single file write due to filesystem buffering and event batching. Unit/Integration on one OS might pass but fail on another. Real filesystem with actual event generation from the OS is necessary.
behaviors.test_description: Watch /tmp/test and log all raw filesystem events from the watcher library (e.g., chokidar events). Write a file and count events. Verify: (1) all events observed are logged, (2) file is processed exactly once despite multiple events. Repeat on multiple OS (Windows, Linux, macOS) to validate.

behaviors.description: If watched directory is removed and recreated while daemon runs, daemon resumes watching without restart
behaviors.justification: Real failure: watcher throws an error when directory is deleted; daemon must catch error and reattach. This depends on OS-specific watcher behavior (how errors are emitted, whether reattach succeeds). Real filesystem operations (rm, mkdir) and real watcher error handling are required.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching /tmp/test. While running, execute `rm -rf /tmp/test && mkdir -p /tmp/test`. Write a new file to /tmp/test and verify daemon picks it up. Observe daemon logs for error recovery. Integration would miss the OS-specific error emission and recovery mechanism.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps
behaviors.justification: Real failure: daemon ignores or misreads creation timestamps (reads modification time instead, or uses wrong timestamp comparison). Unit test of sort logic alone misses if daemon reads wrong timestamp. Integration with real filesystem ensures daemon reads correct creation timestamps and orders correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files rapidly in /tmp/test with known creation order. Verify daemon processes in timestamp order by observing output file sequence or daemon logs with timestamps. Could artificially space creation by 100ms to ensure distinct timestamps on most OSes.

behaviors.description: A file still being written when the arrival event fires is not picked up until writing is complete
behaviors.justification: Real failure: daemon opens/reads a file while it is actively being written to, reading partial/corrupted data. This requires real file handles and I/O in progress. Detecting completion (via file size stability, mtime stability, exclusive open attempt, or filesystem locks) depends on OS file behavior. Mocking file operations would hide the race condition.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching /tmp/test. Open a file for writing (e.g., with keep-alive writes every 10ms). Trigger filesystem event. Verify daemon does not process until writing stops. Could implement via: (1) spawn a background process that writes to file slowly, (2) daemon polls file size/mtime, (3) verify daemon waits for stability before reading.
